title: C# Advanced — Paper 7
description: Advanced C# covering Events & Event Patterns, Async/Await & Task Basics, Task Parallel Library, Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Generics, Expression Trees, Dynamic Types, Advanced Pattern Matching, Records/Tuples/Deconstruction, and Advanced Exception Handling.

questions.text: What is a generic constraint 'where T : IDisposable' used for?

options.text: It forces T to be a value type that can be freed.
It ensures the generic type parameter T implements IDisposable, allowing the generic method/class to call Dispose() on instances of T safely.
It restricts T to sealed classes only.
It makes T a nullable reference type.

questions.text: What is the difference between async Task and async Task<T> return types?

options.text: async Task<T> cannot be awaited; async Task can.
async Task represents an async operation with no result; async Task<T> represents one that produces a result of type T when awaited.
async Task<T> always runs on a background thread; async Task does not.
They are identical and interchangeable.

questions.text: What is the purpose of a ConcurrentBag<T> in parallel code?

options.text: It provides ordered concurrent access like a queue.
It is a thread-safe, unordered collection optimized for scenarios where the same thread both produces and consumes items (bag semantics), avoiding lock contention in parallel loops.
It is a thread-safe sorted set.
ConcurrentBag<T> guarantees FIFO order.

questions.text: What does 'await using' do in C# 8+?

options.text: It disposes the resource on a background thread.
It asynchronously disposes a resource that implements IAsyncDisposable by calling DisposeAsync() and awaiting it, preventing blocking during async cleanup.
It is syntactic sugar for await + using that only works with streams.
It combines await and a using statement for Task objects.

questions.text: What does the ExpressionVisitor.Visit() method do when you do not override a specific Visit method?

options.text: It throws NotSupportedException for unhandled node types.
It returns the node unchanged, effectively acting as an identity transformation for node types you have not overridden.
It removes the unhandled node from the tree.
It calls Compile() on the unhandled node.

questions.text: What is the difference between dynamic and var in C#?

options.text: Both defer type checking to runtime.
var is an implicitly-typed local variable resolved at compile time (still statically typed); dynamic defers all type checking to runtime via the DLR.
var allows runtime member resolution; dynamic does not.
They behave identically for reference types.

questions.text: How does the 'switch expression' with pattern matching handle the default/discard case?

options.text: The 'default' keyword is used in switch expressions.
The discard pattern '_' acts as the catch-all arm in a switch expression, matching anything not handled by earlier arms.
Switch expressions do not support a default case.
Use 'else' as the catch-all arm in a switch expression.

questions.text: What is a primary constructor in C# records and how does it relate to init-only properties?

options.text: A primary constructor is a regular constructor that runs initialization logic.

In records, the primary constructor parameters become init-only auto-implemented properties: 'record Person(string Name, int Age)' generates public string Name { get; init; } and public int Age { get; init; }.
Primary constructors create mutable properties by default.
Primary constructors require explicit property declarations.

questions.text: What does AggregateException.Flatten() do?

options.text: It converts an AggregateException into a single inner exception.
It returns a new AggregateException with all nested AggregateExceptions recursively unwrapped into a single flat list of inner exceptions.
It removes duplicate exceptions from the InnerExceptions list.
It serializes the AggregateException to a flat string.

questions.text: What is a weak event pattern and which class helps implement it in .NET?

options.text: The weak event pattern uses Interlocked to manage subscriptions.
The weak event pattern holds only a WeakReference to subscribers so the publisher does not prevent GC; in .NET, WeakEventManager (WPF) or manual WeakReference<T> can implement this.
A weak event pattern stores event handlers in a HashSet.
WeakEvent is a built-in C# keyword for managing subscriptions.

questions.text: What does Task.Run(async () => ...) return and how is it different from Task.Run(() => ...)?

options.text: Both return Task<T> of the same type.
Task.Run(async () => ...) returns Task<Task> if you pass an async lambda returning Task; you must await it twice or use Task.Run(Func<Task>) which correctly unwraps to a single Task.
They behave identically because Task.Run automatically unwraps.
Task.Run(async () => ...) creates a dedicated thread.

questions.text: What is the purpose of CancellationToken.Register()?

options.text: It registers the token with the operating system scheduler.
It registers a callback to be invoked when cancellation is requested, enabling cleanup actions (like closing a socket) to run when the token is cancelled.
It adds the token to a global cancellation registry.
It links the token to a new CancellationTokenSource.

questions.text: In C# generics, what is an open generic type vs a closed generic type?

options.text: An open generic type is public; a closed generic type is internal.
An open generic type has unbound type parameters (e.g., List<>); a closed generic type has all parameters specified (e.g., List<int>) and can be instantiated.
Open generics can be instantiated; closed generics cannot.
Closed generics are generic interfaces; open generics are classes.

questions.text: What are Parallel.For's return value (ParallelLoopResult) LowestBreakIteration and IsCompleted properties?

options.text: IsCompleted indicates individual task completion; LowestBreakIteration is always 0.
IsCompleted is true if all iterations completed normally; LowestBreakIteration holds the lowest index where Break() was called if the loop was stopped early via ParallelLoopState.Break().
LowestBreakIteration is the total iteration count; IsCompleted is always true.
IsCompleted is always false when using ParallelLoopState.

questions.text: How does record equality work when records have reference-type properties?

options.text: Records use reference equality for all properties.

Record equality uses structural (value) equality: all properties are compared using their own Equals() methods. For reference-type properties, their Equals() is called, which may be reference equality unless overridden.
Records only compare value-type properties; reference-type properties are ignored.
Record equality requires implementing IEquatable<T> manually.

questions.text: What is the difference between try/catch/finally and try/catch when using 'when' filters?

options.text: Exception filters (when) cannot be used with finally blocks.

Exception filters (catch ... when (condition)) are evaluated before the stack unwinds, while catch without a filter unwinds the stack immediately; finally runs regardless of whether a catch is matched.
Exception filters replace finally blocks.
Try/catch/finally and try/catch with 'when' are identical.

questions.text: How does dynamic interop with IronPython or other DLR languages work in C#?

options.text: C# cannot call code from DLR-hosted languages.

The DLR provides a hosting API (ScriptEngine, ScriptScope); C# can use 'dynamic' to call methods on DLR objects, and the DLR handles dispatch between the host (C#) and the dynamic language at runtime.
DLR languages must be compiled to IL before C# can call them.
Only COM interop is supported via 'dynamic'; DLR languages require P/Invoke.

questions.text: What is a slice pattern in C# 11+ list patterns?

options.text: A slice pattern cuts a sequence at a specific index.

The '..' slice pattern within a list pattern matches zero or more elements in a sequence: '[first, .., last]' matches a sequence that starts with first and ends with last regardless of what is in between.
A slice pattern creates a memory slice of the matched sequence.
Slice patterns only work with Span<T> and Memory<T>.

questions.text: What is async stream backpressure and how can it be managed with CancellationToken?

options.text: Async streams do not support backpressure.

Backpressure occurs when the consumer cannot keep up with the producer in an async stream; passing a CancellationToken to the async iterator and checking it allows the consumer to stop production, and using await foreach with EnumeratorCancellation enables cooperative cancellation.
Backpressure in async streams is handled automatically by the runtime.
CancellationToken cannot be used inside async iterators.

questions.text: What is the purpose of Expression.Block() in building expression trees?

options.text: Expression.Block() groups expressions for parallel execution.
Expression.Block() creates a block expression that contains multiple sub-expressions executed sequentially, with the value of the block being the last expression's result.
Expression.Block() is used to wrap exception handling.
Expression.Block() creates a new variable scope.

questions.text: What is the purpose of declaring a delegate type as 'delegate void MyHandler(object sender, EventArgs e)' rather than using a generic Func?

options.text: Named delegate types cannot be used with += and -=.

A named delegate type provides clear semantic intent, appears in IntelliSense with a meaningful name, and follows the .NET event design guideline; Func<object,EventArgs,void> is not valid (void cannot be a type argument).
Named delegates are faster than Func<> at runtime.
Named delegates are required for multicast; Func<> is not multicastable.

questions.text: How do you deconstruct a record with additional non-primary-constructor properties in C# 9+?

options.text: Only primary constructor properties can be deconstructed; extras are ignored.
The compiler generates Deconstruct() only for primary constructor parameters; you can add a custom Deconstruct() method overload to expose additional properties.
All record properties are automatically included in deconstruction.
Records do not support custom Deconstruct overloads.

questions.text: What exception is thrown when you cancel an async operation via CancellationToken?

options.text: CancellationException
OperationCanceledException (or its subclass TaskCanceledException when a Task is cancelled)
TimeoutException
ThreadAbortException

questions.text: What is the unmanaged constraint in C# generics and what types satisfy it?

options.text: It allows T to be any type including managed references.

The 'unmanaged' constraint restricts T to unmanaged types: value types that contain no managed references (e.g., int, double, structs composed entirely of unmanaged types), enabling unsafe pointer operations and stackalloc with T.
Unmanaged constraint restricts to types marked with [Unmanaged] attribute.
It is equivalent to the 'struct' constraint.

questions.text: What does Parallel.ForEach's ParallelLoopState.Break() do?

options.text: It immediately terminates the loop and all already-running iterations.
It signals that no new iterations should start after the current one, but already-started iterations continue; the loop finishes after all iterations with index below the break point complete.
It is equivalent to 'return' in a sequential foreach.
Break() causes the loop to throw a BreakException.

questions.text: What is the 'when' guard in a switch expression arm used for?

options.text: It defines an exception filter for errors inside the arm.
The 'when' guard adds a boolean condition to a switch arm; the arm is selected only if both the pattern matches and the guard condition is true: 'case SomeType x when x.Value > 0 => ...'.
It specifies when (time) the switch expression should execute.
The 'when' guard replaces the pattern in a switch arm.

questions.text: How can ExceptionDispatchInfo be used to marshal exceptions across threads?

options.text: ExceptionDispatchInfo serializes exceptions for cross-thread transfer.

Capture the exception with ExceptionDispatchInfo.Capture(ex) on the faulting thread; store the EDI; call edi.Throw() on the target thread to re-throw the exception with its original stack trace preserved.
ExceptionDispatchInfo automatically propagates exceptions between threads.
ExceptionDispatchInfo is only used for AggregateException unwrapping.

questions.text: What happens if an async iterator method does not have any 'yield return' or 'yield break'?

options.text: The compiler raises an error because IAsyncEnumerable<T> requires at least one yield.
The async iterator is valid; it returns an IAsyncEnumerable<T> that immediately completes with no elements when enumerated.
The method returns null instead of IAsyncEnumerable<T>.
The method runs indefinitely without completing.

questions.text: What is the difference between an EventHandler and a custom delegate for events?

options.text: EventHandler supports multicast; custom delegates do not.

EventHandler<TEventArgs> follows the standard (sender, args) pattern and integrates with .NET tooling; a custom delegate can have any signature but loses standardization and interoperability with frameworks expecting EventHandler.
Custom delegates cannot be used with the event keyword.
EventHandler cannot carry custom data; custom delegates can.

questions.text: What is an immutable record and what makes it 'immutable' in C#?

options.text: A record is immutable if all its fields are private.

A record is considered immutable when its properties are declared with 'init' (or generated from primary constructor parameters), meaning they can only be set at construction time; the 'with' expression creates a new copy rather than mutating the original.
Records are always mutable because C# does not support true immutability.
A record becomes immutable only when sealed.
